content.problemStatement:
A multivalued dependency happens when one attribute determines a set of values, and these values are independent of other attributes. We write this as A ↠ B, meaning "A multidetermines B". Think of it as "one-to-many" relationships where the "many" parts don't affect each other

### Think of it like this:

Imagine an employee in a company. Each employee has various skills (like programming languages they know) and works on different projects. Just because an employee knows Python doesn't mean they must work on the website project, and just because they work on the mobile app doesn't mean they must know Java. Their skills and project assignments are independent of each other



- Employee ID ↠ Skills (Employee E101 knows Python, Java, and SQL)
- Employee ID ↠ Projects (E101 works on Website, Mobile App, and Database projects)

Just because E101 knows Python doesn't mean they must work on any specific project. Their skills and project assignments are independent.

### See how this looks in a database

```sql
CREATE TABLE EmployeeSkills (
EmployeeID VARCHAR(10),
Skill VARCHAR(50),
PRIMARY KEY (EmployeeID, Skill)
);
```

```sql
CREATE TABLE EmployeeProjects (
EmployeeID VARCHAR(10),
Project VARCHAR(50),
PRIMARY KEY (EmployeeID, Project)
);
```

### Key Points to Remember:

- Multivalued dependencies involve sets of values that are determined by a single attribute
- The determined sets must be independent of each other (like skills and projects)
- We use the double arrow (↠) to show multivalued dependencies
- In databases, we often handle these by creating separate tables for each dependent set
- Understanding these helps in achieving Fourth Normal Form (4NF)
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
